our_argument_summary: Our review groups WIN-040 with 8 other V51.0 WINs (040-043, 059-061, 063) under a shared detail_evidence block. The shared text says these 9 WINs reuse INTERMAGNET, NOAA, and Tomsk data already exploited by earlier WINs, and each takes a single data point or ratio and declares it a new WIN. WIN-040 specifically claims the SAA western cell remains west of 45°W. Verdict: Std Model Explains — non-discriminating, dome observes position from same data as WMM with no unique prediction derived from dome geometry.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W022: 'SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated (PENDING)' registered 2026-03-12, test by 2026-03-19
raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-001 (SAA Exponential Cell Separation), WIN-004 and WIN-005 entries cover the same SAA data
monitor.py — NOT present in raw-text directory. Cannot verify monitoring code directly.
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline identified for WIN-040. W022 specifies a position threshold (west of 45°W) and a structural condition (cells separated). No automated monitoring domain has been identified in any prior review. Confirmation appears to be manual/editorial comparison against published WMM maps.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither automated nor computed. W022 was a weekly test registered 2026-03-12 with PENDING status as of our snapshot. No monitoring domain for SAA cell longitude thresholds was found in prior reviews of the dome's 39+ domain mapping.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET (indirectly, as WMM ingests INTERMAGNET station data)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 45°W threshold is arbitrary with no derivation from dome geometry. The 'cells separated' condition specifies no minimum separation, so any non-zero separation satisfies it. Both conditions are designed to be trivially met given current SAA state.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No discrepancies found. Our review makes no specific code claims about WIN-040 individually. The grouped block correctly identifies these as drawing from the same datasets as WIN-004 through WIN-039. Note: monitor.py is not available in the current raw-text snapshot, so direct code inspection remains impossible for Cycle 2 as it was for Cycle 1.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: W022 is a PENDING weekly test prediction with no associated monitoring domain. Prior reviews of WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-039 all confirmed 'none' for their specific monitoring. The dome's monitoring infrastructure includes Domain 8 for generic SAA decay rate at Tsumeb station but nothing for cell longitude thresholds. monitor.py is not in the raw-text archive for independent verification, but the weight of evidence from 39+ domain reviews is conclusive — no SAA longitude monitoring exists.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The SAA two-cell structure and cell positions are standard outputs of NOAA WMM, ESA CHAOS-7, and IGRF models. Cell positions derive from reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary, modeled via magnetohydrodynamics of the outer core (Aubert 2015, Terra-Nova et al. 2017). The dome relabels this as 'aetheric field decay' without changing any numerical values or providing an alternative derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The SAA western cell has been west of 45°W for the entire modern monitoring period (center at ~80°W, tracked continuously since at least 2000). W022 was registered 2026-03-12 — decades after the position was well-established. The cell drifts westward at ~0.2-0.3°/year, making 'remains west of 45°W' trivially true for over a century of extrapolation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is axially symmetric — depends only on radial distance r, not azimuthal angle. SAA cell positions are longitude-specific (American cell ~80°W, African cell ~30°E). The dome model has no mechanism for breaking azimuthal symmetry. The 45°W threshold is not derived from any dome parameter. No derivation path exists from dome geometry to any SAA cell longitude.

holes_found.description: WIN-040 receives no individual analysis in the published review — only the grouped detail_evidence block, which doesn't address the specific 45°W threshold claim. A dome defender could correctly say we dismissed the claim without engaging with it. This is unchanged from Cycle 1; the Cycle 1 recommendation for a WIN-040-specific note does not appear to have been implemented.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_extra for WIN-040 (or to the grouped block): 'WIN-040 claims the SAA western cell remains west of 45°W. The cell center is at approximately 80°W and drifts westward at 0.2-0.3°/year. Setting a threshold 35° east of the cell center ensures automatic satisfaction for over a century — this is not a prediction but a trivially true geographic statement.'

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence block contains a duplicated sentence: 'Notably, the dome's own predictions tracker listed W022 (the prediction corresponding to WIN-040) as PENDING in our raw-text snapshot (2026-03-12), meaning the dome itself had not confirmed this WIN at baseline.' appears twice consecutively. This is a copy-paste error that makes our review look sloppy.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the duplicate sentence. Keep one instance.

holes_found.description: The azimuthal symmetry argument — that H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) cannot predict longitude-dependent features — is a strong structural argument that applies specifically to WIN-040 (and other SAA longitude WINs) but isn't made anywhere in the review. This is a missed opportunity. The dome's equations are radially symmetric; any longitude-specific prediction must be adopted from observations, not derived from dome geometry. This is stronger than 'non-discriminating' because it shows the dome model is structurally incapable of making this type of prediction.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the azimuthal symmetry argument to the grouped block or to individual SAA WINs. Something like: 'The dome's field equation H(r) is axially symmetric and cannot predict longitude-dependent features. SAA cell positions at specific longitudes (American cell ~80°W, African cell ~30°E) require azimuthally non-uniform field structure, which the dome's geometry does not provide.'

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions WIN-061 (Schumann suppression during G3 storms) as an example from this group, but WIN-061 is about Schumann resonance — a completely different topic from SAA cell positions. Including it in a block about 'reusing INTERMAGNET/NOAA data' is technically correct but the jump from SAA geographic position to Schumann ionospheric physics within the same evidence paragraph is jarring. A dome defender could argue we lumped unrelated topics to inflate the 'duplicate' impression.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider whether WIN-061 should be in a separate group or at minimum, ensure the grouped text acknowledges that these 9 WINs span multiple domains (geomagnetic field, pole drift, Schumann resonance) rather than implying they're all SAA-related.

holes_found.description: The verdict 'Std Model Explains' is correct but understates the case for WIN-040 specifically. The 45°W threshold is not just 'explained by standard models' — it's trivially unfalsifiable. A verdict of 'Misleading' (engineered unfalsifiable threshold) could also apply. However, since the Cycle 1 review already assessed this and concluded 'Std Model Explains' captures the core issue, and changing verdict categories has downstream effects on counts and prose, the current verdict is defensible.
holes_found.recommendation: No verdict change recommended. 'Std Model Explains' is sufficient. But if the detail text is expanded per the moderate recommendations above, the 'engineered unfalsifiability' angle should be mentioned to let readers see both dimensions.

stronger_arguments.description: Quantify the unfalsifiability: The SAA western cell center is at ~80°W, drifting westward at 0.2-0.3°/year. For the cell to cross 45°W eastward (falsifying WIN-040), the drift would need to reverse and the cell would need to travel 35° — at maximum historical drift rates, this would take over 100 years. This makes WIN-040 less a prediction and more a statement about current geography.
sources: NOAA WMM2025 — current SAA western cell center longitude
Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125 — SAA drift rates

stronger_arguments.description: The axially symmetric dome geometry argument: H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) has no azimuthal dependence. It literally cannot produce longitude-specific predictions from first principles. Any SAA longitude claim from the dome model is necessarily adopted from external observations. This is a stronger structural argument than 'non-discriminating' because it shows the dome model is mathematically incapable of this class of prediction, not merely that it happens to agree with standard models.
sources: Dome's own model parameters page (05-model.txt) — H(r) equation
Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543-3548 — longitude-dependent SAA structure from CMB flux patches

kernel_of_truth.description: The SAA two-cell structure is real, scientifically important, and not trivial to identify. The western (American) cell is indeed west of 45°W with center at ~80°W. The dome model's author has engaged with real geomagnetic data about SAA morphology, which is more than most pseudoscientific models attempt. Recognizing the dual-cell structure and its spatial properties shows familiarity with current geomagnetic research.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three problems: (1) The dome's axially symmetric field equation cannot derive longitude-specific features — the 45°W value must be adopted from published data. (2) The 45°W threshold is 35° east of the cell center, making it trivially unfalsifiable for over a century. (3) Standard core dynamics models (WMM, CHAOS-7, IGRF) already predict SAA cell positions from first-principles MHD of the outer core. Knowing the observation is real does not constitute predicting it from a model.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 re-review of WIN-040. Comparing against Cycle 1 review (2026-04-06):

**Text Changes Since Cycle 1**: The text_fingerprint comparison shows the detail_evidence is at 917 characters. Cycle 1 did not record a fingerprint (was the first review), so I'm establishing the baseline for Cycle 3. The key concern from Cycle 1 — that the grouped treatment lacks WIN-040-specific analysis — appears NOT to have been addressed. The detail_evidence block is unchanged.

**New Finding: Duplicate Sentence**: On close re-reading, the detail_evidence contains a verbatim duplicate sentence about W022 being PENDING. This is a copy-paste error that was not caught in Cycle 1 review. This is flagged as moderate severity — it's cosmetic but makes our review look unpolished.

**Cycle 1 Fix Assessment**: The Cycle 1 review recommended adding a WIN-040-specific note about the trivial 45°W threshold and the azimuthal symmetry problem. This recommendation has not been implemented — the grouped block remains generic. The Cycle 1 review recommended checking whether W022 was confirmed in a newer snapshot — this also appears not to have been acted on, though the raw-text archive has not been updated since 2026-04-05.

**Code Analysis Tags Re-validation**: All four tags from Cycle 1 are reconfirmed:
- monitoring: 'none' — correct. No monitoring domain for SAA cell longitude exists.
- relabels_standard: true — correct. SAA cell positions are WMM/CHAOS-7 outputs.
- post_hoc: true — correct. Western cell has been at ~80°W for 25+ years.
- derives_from_dome: false — correct. H(r) is axially symmetric; cannot produce longitude predictions.

**monitor.py Still Unavailable**: As in Cycle 1, monitor.py is not in the raw-text directory. This is a data gap, but the convergence of evidence from 39+ domain reviews by prior curmudgeon cycles makes the 'none' classification highly confident.

**Verdict Assessment**: 'Std Model Explains' remains correct. The Cycle 1 review considered whether 'Misleading' would be more appropriate (given the engineered unfalsifiability of the 45°W threshold) and concluded 'Std Model Explains' captures the core issue. I agree — the primary problem is non-discrimination (standard models explain the same observation), not deception per se. The unfalsifiability is a secondary concern best addressed in the detail text.

**Priority Items for Decider**:
1. Fix the duplicate sentence in detail_evidence (moderate, easy fix)
2. Add WIN-040-specific text about the 45°W threshold triviality and azimuthal symmetry problem (moderate, would strengthen the rebuttal)
3. Consider whether the WIN-061 grouping with SAA WINs is coherent (minor, structural)
